schools.summary: Bede: the women came at first light with spices, restrained till the Sabbath passed; Athanasius: he rose the third day to prove his death real, lest any say he never died.
Cyril: woman, once the minister of death, is now first to tell the mystery of the resurrection.
'At early dawn' (orthrou batheos, deep dawn; John 20:1 'yet dark'); Henry distinguishes the hidden manner from the revealed, infallible proofs of the rising.

themes: the first day of the week

schools.summary: 'Rolled away' (apokekylismenon, perfect passive); Luke's less vivid report perhaps from Joanna/Susanna; the stone the women worried over found already removed.
Clarke: an angel rolled it away (Matt 28:2) — those who set out in faith may leave obstacles to God, who removes them; the stone moved to let witnesses in, not the Lord out.

'Of the Lord Jesus' a Western non-interpolation (one of several in this chapter); the women entered the spacious tomb and found no body — the first evidence of the resurrection, though not yet understood.
Clarke: 'the body' named precisely — only it was subject to death, the soul having been in Paradise (Luke 23:43).
The harmony: Luke and John note two angels, Mark and Matthew the one who spoke — the natural variation of different witnesses; 'two men' in shining garments, heavenly interpreters of the empty tomb.

debates.question: One angel at the tomb or two?

sides.position: two were present; Mark and Matthew note only the one who spoke

schools.summary: 'Why seek ye the living among the dead?' — better 'Him that liveth,' alive for evermore (Rev 1:18); the question changes the whole current of their thoughts; they had forgotten his prediction of rising.

themes: 'why seek the living among the dead?'

schools.summary: 'He is not here, but is risen' (a Western non-interpolation, genuine in Mark/Matthew); the appeal to memory peculiar to Luke — he plainly foretold the third-day rising (Luke 9:22; 18:33), which they had forgotten.

themes: 'he is not here, but is risen'

schools.summary: The recalled prophecy — the Son of man delivered to 'sinful men' (the Gentile Romans, John 19:15; cf 'sinners of the Gentiles,' Gal 2:15), crucified, risen the third day — now fulfilled to the letter.

themes: 'the Son of man must be delivered'

schools.summary: Clarke: the recollection of Christ's words a source of comfort, the words of eternal life; their memories refreshed and understandings opened.
Calvin: though they profited little, the doctrine was not lost but choked up, until in due time it yielded fruit — the word dormant, not wasted.
They 'told' (apengeilan) — a wonderful proclamation — the eleven and all the rest; the women the first heralds of the resurrection, returning in fear and joy.

The named witnesses — Mary Magdalene, Joanna (wife of Herod's steward, Luke 8:3, named by Luke alone, likely his informant), Mary mother of James — giving the resurrection the weight of identifiable firsthand testimony.

'Idle tales' (leros, only here; medical word for delirious babble); they 'kept on distrusting' (imperfect) — the apostles' flat disbelief, which (being reluctant) strengthens the evidence, the resurrection overcoming stubborn doubt in its first witnesses.

Peter (with John, who arrived first, John 20:3) runs to the tomb; the linen clothes 'laid by themselves' a proof of an orderly rising, not a stolen body; the verse a Western non-interpolation; he departs 'wondering,' not yet believing.
Calvin: Luke inverts the order, the running having preceded; Eusebius: Peter of more ardent feeling than the rest.
Gregory: the Lord appeared in an unrecognised form, mirroring outwardly their inward love-mixed-with-doubt; Augustine reads hospitality leading to knowledge of him.
Two disciples walk ~7 miles (sixty stadia) on resurrection day; the unnamed companion debated (Luke himself? Peter, cf 1 Cor 15:5?), Cleopas named in v18; the narrative peculiar to Luke.

debates.question: Who was the unnamed second disciple on the Emmaus road?

sides.position: possibly Luke himself, modestly concealing his name

schools.summary: They 'communed' (homiloun, root of 'homiletics,' preaching first conversational) of all that had happened — the cross, tomb, and reports turned over in sorrow.
Calvin: a proof of godliness that they cherished their faith, small and weak, as a shield against the offence of the cross — holy conversation in doubt, to which Christ draws near.
'Jesus himself' (autos Iesous) in actual person drew near and walked with them while they reasoned of him; they were devoutly employed, and to such he came — neither traveller of the Twelve.
Barnes/Clarke: nothing miraculous — he 'appeared in another form' (Mark 16:12); their eyes 'continued held' from recognising him fully.
Calvin: a real restraint on their sight, lest they think his body changed — he remained himself, but their eyes were held until the appointed moment.

debates.question: Why did the disciples not recognise the risen Lord?

sides.position: a natural cause — he appeared 'in another form'
a real restraint laid on their sight for a purpose

schools.summary: 'That ye have with another' (antiballete, throwing words back and forth like a ball); the better text adds 'they stood still, looking sad'; he draws out their grief by asking of it.
Calvin: as then, so daily — Christ approaches unperceived to instruct, leading us to voice our trouble.

Cleopas (a Greek contraction, distinct from Clopas of John 19:25) marvels that the stranger ('dost thou alone sojourn?' paroikeis) had not heard of events so public — the irony that the stranger is their centre.
'A prophet mighty in deed and word' — their estimate high but incomplete; they believed him a prophet and hoped he would redeem Israel, but had not risen to confessing him the Christ, the Son of God.

themes: a prophet mighty in deed and word

schools.summary: 'Delivered him to a sentence of death' — the Sanhedrin condemned on a capital charge but handed him to Pilate for execution (Acts 13:27); the crucifixion the wound at the centre of the disciples' grief.
'But we hoped' (imperfect, emphatic) he would redeem Israel — a largely earthly hope of release from Rome; Cleopas' confused, broken speech; the poignant 'third day' unrecognised.
Calvin: their hope was not wholly broken off, though their words seem so — choked and bewildered, not extinguished.

themes: 'we trusted he should have redeemed Israel'

schools.summary: The women 'amazed us' (exestesan, root of 'ecstasy'), early at the tomb — astonishment, not faith; the disciples knew the report yet walked still in sadness.

They report the women's 'vision of angels' (the word used of Zacharias, Luke 1:22) saying 'he was alive' — but this was 'too indirect and uncertain' for the disciples; the gospel repeated without belief.
'Certain of them' (Peter and John, John 20:3) found the tomb empty 'even so as the women said' — an undesigned coincidence with John's report; but 'him they saw not,' the evidence short of sight.
Theophylact: he reproves their excess of doubt, slow to believe 'all' the prophets — possible to believe some and not all; the suffering as well as the glory.

'Fools' (anoetoi, 'without sense,' not the harsher term of Matt 5:22; Paul's word for the 'foolish Galatians,' Gal 3:1) — a rebuke of dulness, not a curse; the fault unbelief of Scripture, not want of evidence.

The Christ 'must' suffer and so enter his glory — a divine necessity, the cross the appointed road to the crown; the very sufferings that disturbed them the strongest proof of the Messiah (1 Pet 1:11).

'Beginning at Moses and all the prophets, he expounded' (diermeneusen, root of 'hermeneutics') — a full opening of all Scripture concerning himself; the whole OT a witness to the suffering and glorified Christ.
Calvin: Christ made known through the Law and the Prophets — he himself the best expositor, founding the gospel on the proof of the older Scriptures.

themes: 'beginning at Moses and all the prophets'

schools.summary: Barnes: Christ kept on as if to go further, and would have, had they not constrained him; Clarke: his discourse had warmed their hearts (v.32), and they counted it a privilege to entertain the preacher — the doctrine of Christ making the heart entreat its Author to abide; Christ drawing out their desire by seeming to depart (as with the Canaanite woman, Matthew 15:23-28), rewarding the earnest longing that constrains him to stay.

themes: he made as though he would have gone further

schools.summary: Gill: 'constrained' not force but urgent importunity, taking no denial; Barnes: charmed by his discourse, they longed to hear more, the believer's chief pleasure fellowship with the Saviour; the holy violence of longing prevailing with Christ (Genesis 32:26), who yields to their entreaty and goes in to abide — the abiding presence granted to importunate desire ('Abide with us').

themes: the abiding presence granted to desire

schools.summary: Lightfoot argues this was not the Eucharist but the ordinary blessing of a meal — Christ vanished in distributing the bread, ending the supper before it began, none said to have eaten, using a common grace not 'This is my body'; Christ, as host, blessing and breaking bread in his familiar manner, the occasion of their recognizing him — Christ revealed in the ordinary acts of grace, not only extraordinary signs.

debates.question: Was the breaking of bread at Emmaus the Eucharist?

sides.position: No — the ordinary blessing of a meal (Christ vanished in distributing it, none ate), not the sacrament

themes: he took bread, and blessed, and brake
not the Eucharist but ordinary grace

schools.summary: Gill: 'their eyes were opened' — not blind before, but the restraint that held them removed (perhaps the familiar action stirring memory); Barnes: the obscurity gone, they knew him the Messiah, doubts dispelled, then he suddenly withdrew; the sovereign opening of the eyes by which Christ is known (the spiritual parallel), and the vanishing teaching them to live by faith not sight in the risen Lord.

themes: their eyes were opened, and they knew him
he vanished out of their sight
the sovereign opening of the eyes
living by faith in the risen Lord

schools.summary: Lightfoot notes the textual variant ('was our heart veiled?') but prefers the received sense — the burning heart compared to Psalm 39:3 and Jeremiah 20:9, their hearts so warmed they could hold no longer; the kindling power of Christ's opening of the Scriptures (showing how they testify of him, v.27), the inward witness of his presence felt before seen — the word of Christ setting the heart aflame.

themes: did not our heart burn within us
the kindling power of the word
the inward witness of his presence

schools.summary: Gill: though it was evening and they were wearied, being full of joy at Christ's appearance they rose at once and hastened back to Jerusalem; Barnes: their joy could not be kept, hastening to tell others (as Andrew and Philip, John 1:41-45; Psalm 66:16) — the self-denying eagerness of those who have found the Saviour, and the gathered fellowship of disciples receiving the converging testimonies.

themes: they rose up the same hour

schools.summary: Lightfoot: these are the words of the eleven greeting the two from Emmaus, confessing the resurrection as certain ('risen indeed') confirmed by the appearance to Peter; the risen Christ appearing first (of the apostles) to Simon Peter, who had denied him (also in 1 Corinthians 15:5) — the fruit of Christ's prayer (Luke 22:32) and a token of pardoning grace, seeking and restoring the fallen disciple.

Gill: the two recounted how Jesus joined them, opened the Scriptures, and was known in breaking bread; Ellicott: Luke's 'breaking of bread' had acquired a settled meaning (Acts 2:42), though the Emmaus act was the ordinary meal (Lightfoot, v.30); the convergence of testimonies (empty tomb, angels, Peter, Emmaus) building the assured confession, Christ known in breaking bread, the mutual witness strengthening the gathered church.

themes: told what things were done in the way

schools.summary: Chrysostom: as the disciples longed to see him, the much-desired Lord comes with the clearest evidence; Augustine harmonizes with John — Thomas absent (John 20:24), 'the eleven' used of the apostolic company; Christ stands suddenly (doors shut, John 20:19) greeting them 'Peace be unto you' — the peace of his finished work (John 14:27), the risen Lord coming with grace not rebuke to his troubled, seeking people.

themes: Jesus himself stood in the midst
the peace of his finished work

schools.summary: Gill: terrified at his sudden appearance (doors shut, no sound), unable to account for it though they had spoken of his resurrection; Lightfoot: the Jewish notion of 'spirits' (souls without bodies, apparitions of the dead), supposing his ghost not his body; the slowness of faith showing the disciples no easy dupes — the resurrection forced on them by evidence against their fears, giving occasion for fuller proof.

themes: supposed they had seen a spirit
the apologetic value of their doubt

schools.summary: Barnes: 'thoughts' = doubts/suspicions, Christ reproving their doubt when Scripture, his own word, and the testimonies (Peter, the angels) had given every ground to believe; Ellicott: the question witnesses to the risen Lord's identity of character with his humanity; the tender reproof (not harsh) of unreasonable doubt against abundant evidence, the same Jesus understanding and calming their fears.

Gill: the prints of the nails (and side, John 20:20) proving he was no spirit but risen in the same numerical body that suffered; Barnes: Christ appealed to sight and touch, acts a disembodied spirit could not do — 'a spirit hath not flesh and bones'; the reality and identity of the risen body (against docetism), the foundation of the resurrection hope, the enduring wounds proving both his identity and his love.

themes: behold my hands and my feet
a spirit hath not flesh and bones
the reality of the bodily resurrection

schools.summary: Gill: Christ put them in the way of satisfying themselves by their senses, showing his hands and feet; Robertson notes the verse a 'Western non-interpolation' (Westcott-Hort) but the showing genuine in John 20:20; the condescension of Christ furnishing visible proof of the risen body (its reality and identity), meeting weak faith with full evidence — the displayed wounds the tokens of accomplished redemption.

themes: he shewed them his hands and his feet
visible proof of the risen body

schools.summary: Cyril: Christ, having shown his hands and feet, asked for food to confirm them still more; the tender 'believed not for joy' — joy too great to credit (as the women slept 'for sorrow,' Luke 22:45), the bewilderment of gladness not hardened unbelief; Gregory of Nyssa: the resurrection meal sweetened (the honeycomb) signifying the joy after the bitter herbs of suffering — Christ adding proof to proof for weak faith.

Ellicott: the food the common fare of the disciples — fish the staple (the feedings, John 21:9), honey common in the land 'flowing with milk and honey'; Gregory of Nyssa: the contrast of the Passover (bitter herbs, bondage's bitterness) with the resurrection meal sweetened by the honeycomb (the joy of the risen life); the homely reality proving the risen body, the glorified Christ sharing the disciples' ordinary table.

themes: a piece of a broiled fish, and honeycomb
the sweetness of the risen life

schools.summary: Bede: Christ ate 'not from necessity but by His power' (the earth absorbs water from want, the sun from power) — his glorified body needing no food yet truly eating to prove its reality; Calvin: the condescension bearing with the disciples' weakness, joining the common usages of mortals though risen to heavenly life; the eating the conclusive proof of the bodily resurrection (Acts 10:41), against every denial.

debates.question: Why did the risen Christ eat, if his glorified body needed no food?

sides.position: By power not necessity — to prove the reality of his body and confirm weak faith

themes: he took it, and did eat before them
the conclusive proof of the resurrection

schools.summary: Lightfoot: 'the law of Moses, the prophets, and the psalms' = the threefold Hebrew canon (Law, Prophets, Writings, named by the Psalms); Christ appeals to the whole Old Testament as testifying of him, all that was written 'must be fulfilled' — his sufferings and glory foreshadowed in the law, foretold by the prophets, sung in the psalms (Revelation 19:10); the unity of Scripture, Christ its theme and substance.

themes: the law of Moses, the prophets, and the psalms
the whole OT testifies of Christ

schools.summary: Bede: the progression — seen/handled, the Scriptures recalled, then the understanding opened; Theophylact: necessary, else their perplexed minds could not learn the mystery of Christ (1 Corinthians 2:14); Calvin: against those who make outward doctrine superfluous — Christ both speaks the word and opens the mind, both needed; the saving understanding of Scripture Christ's gift, the inward light joined to the outward word.

debates.question: Is the outward word enough, or is inward illumination needed?

sides.position: Both — Christ gives the Scriptures AND opens the understanding; neither alone suffices

themes: that they might understand the scriptures
the word joined to the inward light

schools.summary: Barnes: 'it behoved' = became necessary/fitting that the Messiah suffer and rise, predicted (Isaiah 53:3-5; Acts 4:12) and required for redemption; Robertson: Christ finds in the OT his suffering, resurrection, and the gospel commission; the cross not defeat but the foretold and appointed way of salvation, the rising the foretold victory — comforting the disciples that his death was Scripture's fulfilment, not failure.

themes: thus it behoved Christ to suffer

schools.summary: Gill: repentance and remission the sum of the gospel, joined as gifts of grace but not as cause and effect — repentance no satisfaction for sin, remission procured solely by Christ's blood (the meritorious cause), both preached 'in his name'; the universal reach to all nations, 'beginning at Jerusalem' the grace offering pardon first to Christ's murderers (Acts 2), the gospel spreading from the center outward (Acts 1:8).

themes: preached in his name among all nations

schools.summary: Gill: the apostles witnesses of Christ's humanity, miracles, sufferings, death, and above all his resurrection (shown by many infallible proofs, Acts 1:3), and to testify repentance and remission to all nations; Barnes: witnesses of his life, sufferings, resurrection; the gospel founded on credible eyewitness testimony (1 John 1:3), the apostles' high office sealed even with their blood ('witness' = martys).

themes: ye are witnesses of these things
the foundation of the gospel testimony

schools.summary: Gill: 'the promise of my Father' = the Holy Spirit (promised by the Father, poured out in Messiah's times), coming at Pentecost ten days later in fire (Acts 2:3); the apostles to 'tarry' until 'endued with power from on high' — clothed and qualified for their witness; the necessity of the Spirit's power for the work, the duty of waiting, and dependence on God's enabling for all true service.

themes: tarry until endued with power from on high

schools.summary: Bede: Luke joins the resurrection day to the ascension (omitting the forty days, Acts 1:3); Bethany ('house of obedience') fitting — he who descended for the disobedience of the wicked ascends for the obedience of the converted, the Church founded on Christ; Theophylact: the blessing poured a power of preservation until the Spirit; Christ's last act a priestly blessing (Leviticus 9:22), the ascended Lord ever blessing his people.

themes: led them out as far as Bethany
lifted up his hands and blessed them

schools.summary: Clarke: Christ carried up to the heaven he had descended from (John 3:13), forty days after the resurrection (Acts 1:3), having given convincing proofs (seen by 500+, 1 Corinthians 15:6) — the fullest proof of the soul's immortality, the body's resurrection, and his continual intercession; Ellicott: 'and carried up into heaven' absent from some best MSS, though the ascension is firm in Acts 1:9-11; Christ ascending as forerunner, High Priest, and King.

debates.question: Are the words 'and carried up into heaven' original in Luke?

sides.position: Absent from some of the best MSS and omitted by some editors, though the ascension is firmly attested elsewhere (Acts 1:9-11)

schools.summary: Gill: the disciples worshipped Christ with religious worship as God, confirmed in his deity and divine sonship by the resurrection and ascension; Barnes: worship offered to an absent Saviour after he left — an act of religion, the first worship of the ascended Christ; the deity of Christ witnessed by the worship of former Jews zealous against idolatry, and the great joy (not grief) at his exaltation, the days of mourning ended (Isaiah 60:20).

themes: returned to Jerusalem with great joy

schools.summary: Gill: continually in the temple at the hours of worship, and in the upper room, with one accord in prayer (Acts 1:14); Barnes: until Pentecost (Acts 2), praising God for the proof that the Messiah had come, redeemed, and ascended — 'the days of their mourning ended' (Isaiah 60:20); obeying Christ's charge to wait (v.49); Luke's Gospel framed in temple-worship and praise (opening in Luke 1), closing with adoration and 'Amen.'

themes: the joyful close of the Gospel
